original_abstract:
While the goal of any "omic" science is the comprehensive measurement of its constituents, achieving full coverage in metabolomics remains a formidable challenge due to the chemical diversity of small molecules. Conventional isolation and separation strategies often introduce significant bias; reversed-phase liquid chromatography (RPLC) favors lipophilic compounds, while hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography (HILIC) is tailored for water-soluble metabolites. In this study, we developed an integrated platform that bridges these two approaches to expand metabolome coverage.

We first optimized an aminopropyl-based HILIC/MS method by evaluating various mobile-phase additives and pH conditions to ensure high reproducibility. When applied to untargeted profiling of perturbed bacterial metabolomes, this HILIC method successfully captured critical dysregulated metabolites in central carbon pathways—including amino acids, organic acids, phosphorylated sugars, and energy metabolites—that were poorly retained by RPLC. To demonstrate the versatility of our integrated workflow, we analyzed bacterial cells, human plasma, and cancer cells using a single extraction protocol (80% organic solvent). 

By combining RPLC and HILIC in both ESI positive and negative modes, we detected over 30,000 features per sample type, spanning both lipid and central carbon metabolism. Our results show that RPLC in ESI positive mode and HILIC in ESI negative mode provided the highest number of unique features. Ultimately, we demonstrate that when sample volume and instrument time are constrained, the combination of RPLC (+) and HILIC (–) provides the most comprehensive biological snapshot of the metabolome.
humanized_abstract:
While the ultimate goal of any “omic” discipline is to comprehensively quantify its constituents, the fact that small molecules exhibit an almost infinite degree of chemical diversity renders metabolomics with full coverage of the metabolome a far from accomplished goal. Current procedures, which depend on selective isolation and separation of the analytes, such as reversed-phase liquid chromatography (RPLC), which favors lipophilic analytes, and hydrophilic interaction liquid chromatography (HILIC), which favors hydrophilic species, introduce considerable bias. Here, we show that by using an integrated platform that complements the biases of each individual chromatographic mode, thereby affording the most extended possible metabolome coverage.

An aminopropyl-silica based HILIC/MS method was initially optimized by optimizing the effects of several different additives in the mobile phase as well as pH and ensured high precision. This method was then applied to an untargeted metabolic profile assessment of a perturbed bacterial metabolome. Many key metabolites in central carbon metabolism involved in biosynthesis of amino acids, organic acids, phosphorylated sugars and energy metabolites that had low or non-existent ionization in RPLC were poorly retained. To exemplify the flexibility and multi-faceted capabilities of our approach, the integrated workflow was applied to separate analysis of bacterial cells, human plasma and cancer cells by using a common extraction protocol (80% MeOH).

Using a parallel approach that involves the combination of RPLC and HILIC in both ESI positive and negative modes, we were able to identify over 30,000 features per sample type encompassing lipids and central carbon metabolism. We determined that the best orthogonal sets of features are obtained with RPLC in ESI positive mode and HILIC in ESI negative mode and we conclude that the best practical approach when sample volumes are limited and when time on the UHPLC system is limited, is the use of RPLC (+) and HILLC (–).
